speaker: interviewer
text: The date is December 4, 1995. The survivor is Nathan Krieger. The interviewer is Rivka Wakslak. The city is Cedarhurst, New York, in the United States of America. The language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Nathan Krieger. I am Rivka Wakslak. We will be conducting this interview in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Krieger, thank you so much for participating in the project. Can you tell me your full name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Nathan Krieger. I was born February the 21st, 1917, in Wisnicz Nowy, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell your name for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-R-I-E-G-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I'm 78.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Burek Krieger. My mother's name was Malka. Her maiden name was Beldengreen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any sisters or brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had six sisters and two brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest sister was Mecha. Then was Chana, then was Sarah. Then was my brother Joseph, my sister Mirel, and was me, and was my younger brother, Moshe. And I had a sister Mindel, and the younger one was Feigel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about your family. What did your father do for a living? I--

speaker: interviewee
text: --don't remember my father. My father left Europe when I was 5 years old. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he go?

speaker: interviewee
text: He went to America. And what he did here, I really don't know exactly. But he died in 1927.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 10 years old. But I never-- I don't remember my father, because I was 5 years old when he left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father send money to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he sent money to our family. He also sent $10,000 dowry for my older sister to marry the boy what he knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's a lot of money.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was successful in America.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was very successful in America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he planning to bring your family there-- here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. When I came here to this country, my cousin-- my father's sister's son-- I met him and he told me the story that he had papers for all of us. And he's supposed to come to Europe to attend the wedding of my sister, and then bring the whole family to the United States. And something happened on the boat what he's supposed to come. And that was before Rosh Hashanah. And he was a very Orthodox man, and he was afraid that he will have to travel during the holidays, so he postponed. And he decided to come after the holidays. Unfortunately, he never made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a fire in the building, and he was burned. They took him to the hospital, and he died the first day of Rosh Hashanah. It was in 1927.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your family after he died? How did your mother manage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it wasn't easy. She remained with nine children. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were very well off while my father was alive, and then everything changed. One child raised the other. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your family, how you lived while-- while your father was alive and sending you money. Tell me about your home then. How were things before he died?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't remember much. I was 7, 8, 9, years old. But I know we were very well off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you move after he died? Did you have to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Before he went to America he bought a house for us in Wisnicz Nowy, and he also bought a house for my mother's parents, so we lived next to each other. So my-- I remember my grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe your home? How big was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had two rooms and a kitchen. And there were nine children living.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the two rooms. So it was a--

speaker: interviewee
text: But we were very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a small-- it was a small house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] As I said, one child, one sister, brother. The older sisters start to work. They made shirts. And-- --that's how the family was raised.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the oldest daughters helped support the family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I remember my older brother was a tailor. I remember bringing him breakfast in the morning. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I used to help him before I went to school. Used to help him for half an hour, an hour or so. By the end of the week he gave me a zloty, whatever, so I had some pocket money.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I used to come home from school. My sisters used to teach me how to sew on a machine. I was 10 years old and I knew already how to sew on a machine. And then when I was 12 years old, I was bar mitzvahed. So my mother gave me away for four years to learn the trade-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --as a tailor. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But when I came in there, usually the custom was you used to clean the house and everything. But I knew how to sew on machine, so I was valuable for my boss. And he put me on a machine right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this boss?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a very, very nice people, very Orthodox man. He-- he only spoke Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in Wisnicz?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Wisnicz, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live at home then, or you lived with the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived at home, yeah. Well, that was across the street from my house. I was there for two years, and I was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --I had the knack to tailoring, and I was very good in it. I wanted to leave after two years, 2 and 1/2 years. I begged my mother. I could go and earn 25 zlotys already, which was a lot of money, because there was no food in the house and everything. There were younger brothers and sisters. My mother said, I made a deal for four years. In those days, whatever your mother said, it went. So I worked there for the four years. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we helped to raise the younger children and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell, me, what was the yontiff like? Your family was a religious family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my family was very-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --very religious, my mother, my grandfather. My father came here to this country, and he belonged to-- it was a very Orthodox congregation on the east side, the 99. Somebody told me about it here. He davened there. He was like the [INAUDIBLE]. And he learned the Torah. He was a very learned man, because my grandfather was very well to do before World War I. He went to a yeshiva and he picked up the best learned man for-- because my grandfather had five sons and one daughter. And my mother was the only daughter. So he picked up the chazan for my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at home, when you were growing up, how did you celebrate the holidays? Did you stay with your grandparents? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, with my mother, with the family. My grandparents died when I was-- when I was young. I remember them, but I remember they lived, you know, just across from us. And we celebrate Shabbos and yontiff and all the holidays, the family together. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And then my oldest sister-- You were close to this sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very close. We were a very close family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which sister was this? Which sister were you close to?

speaker: interviewee
text: To all of them. To all of them. We were-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --like, one child, you listen to an older sister. You listen to an older brother. You listen to your mother. It's-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --then when I was 12 years old, I started to be on my own already. I went to work. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Got up 6:00. 7:00 you had to be at work. Then I had to go-- the law was I had to go to night school, so I left from 5:00 till 8:00.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which school was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in Wisnicz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- this was high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't high school, actually. It was similar to high school, like a training school. And-- but the bad part was, 8:00, I had to go back and make up the three hours. And i worked till 11:00. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Went home hungry to sleep. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was the life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you learn in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was very good in school. I was very good in elementary school. School was the best thing for me, because I had a lot of Polish friends, which actually they didn't know how to read and how to write. And I used to help them with their homework and with everything. And so one used to bring me bread. The other one used to bring me some butter, the other one some fruit, which that was the best thing. I had something to eat, and I brought home something for-- for the family, they should have something to eat. And that went on for three years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of things-- did you have time to play when you were working?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I got up 6:00, 6:30. I came home 11:00, 11:30 at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any anti-Semitism while you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of things happened that made you feel that people didn't like Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, remember the teacher-- I never got the grade what I deserved because I was Jewish. I remember the guys what I used to make the homework for them, they got better grades. Without my help, they would never pass a grade.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone say anything to you that this is because you're Jewish, you don't get something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had-- as I was older a little bit, we had problems with the guys, the Gentiles, because it was like-- --I think it was around-- I think it was half Jewish and half Gentiles. But they felt that they had the majority and it's their country. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And it was Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong to any political organizations? Any Zionist organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I belonged to the Zionist organization, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get together with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, sure, we got together every night.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get together? What time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if I didn't-- the nights what I didn't have to go to school, because I think it was, if my memory is correct, it was three times a week. So we used to go-- 7:00 we got together. And we were there till about 10:00, 10:30. We learned Hebrew, Jewish history. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we really had-- that was our social life. And everybody was looking forward.

speaker: interviewer
text: To what?

speaker: interviewee
text: To get together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you want to leave the country? Did you want to go to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I went to a [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What's that?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was-- that was preparing to go to Israel to work, you know, like the halutzim in those days. But they took my older brother to the Polish army.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: My older brother was born in 1912. He was 21 years old, so must have been--

speaker: interviewee
text: --1933, '34. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah, so I was the next man in the house. And I-- I couldn't leave, because probably, if they wouldn't take my older brother, I would go to Palestine, to Israel. How long was he in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in the army over a year. When he had a toothache once there, and was a Jewish dentist in the army there, so he start to ask him where he's from, and how's the family and everything. And he told him that, you know, there's no father, and there are nine children in the house. And the dentist must be a very nice guy, and he said, if you allow me to pull out one more tooth from your mouth, I could release you from the army, because the law was you had to have so many teeth. And he said, of course, why not? And he came home.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he was in the army. I used to work at night, the kind of hours to make a few extra zlotys. And  I promised him that I'm going to send him five zlotys a week that he should have something extra to eat and everything. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's how it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where there any anti-Semitic laws that were going on in Poland before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. There were signs already on the stores. You know, don't buy by a Jewish storekeeper.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war. Don't buy by a Jewish storekeeper. Come and-- I remember was an incident, we were walking from the Zionist organization, and it was before Christmas. And they started to beat up the Jews. So they came and they run, and my brother was in the army. For a soldier, they had to have respect. And he came out and he pulled out his sabre. And they all ran away, because once you start up with a soldier, he was allowed to kill you. You're not allowed to touch a soldier. We had plenty-- plenty of incidents there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did things change when the war started? When Germany--

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean by change?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did things change? How did your life change when the war started?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my sister was married--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: --about that time. Two sisters were married before the war. And one sister moved away to a small town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which town?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- [NON-ENGLISH] it was called.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did she move to [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her husband came from there, and they had some kind of little farm. And so it was a little bit easier. Used to go and buy some-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --jackets or pants, and go out there and exchange by the Polacks, because you couldn't buy anything to wear, for flour, or for butter, or potatoes. And this is how we survived. And then my third sister married. She was a beautiful girl, and she married a guy from our town. His name was Shlomo Pincusfeld.

speaker: interviewer
text: She married during the war? When the war--

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war. And they were very well off, so she helped us a lot. So between the two sisters, when they took my older brother right away to work, and I used to go around and exchange stuff to have food. That how we were interested, because we had the house. We lived in the house. And should be able to bring some potatoes and bread. That's what you prayed for. If you had potatoes and bread, you had everything. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is how we lived till 1942. 1942, they made Judenrein. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we had to leave our houses and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your mother--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother went to my sister in that small town there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the younger sisters and my older brother were at that time in the ghetto in Bochnia, working.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother hide any of her belongings? Any precious things?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have too much to hide, because whatever we had from before-- from the good days, since my father died, everything was gone. In order to survive, you sold everything, what valuable stuff what we had to have bread on the table. And my mother went there, and-- --I was there with her, because my wife's parents went also to hiding. And they were not too far away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you married then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were friendly with your wife to be?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we kept company.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were dating her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was dating. And we were supposed to get married, but the war broke out. So we postponed it in 1939 till 1942. And I knew my mother was there, so we went there. And then after a short time, they gave away all the money, my wife's parents. And the guy said to them, sorry, you have to leave. They were there a few days. And you can't stay here, because they're going around and looking around who hide Jews. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --they'll kill you, and they'll kill us. And the same thing happened where my mother was hidden. But the guy was--

speaker: interviewer
text: So when they declared that all the Jews had to go to Bochnia, your mother and you went into hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did-- and she was being hidden by--

speaker: interviewee
text: By that-- by a Polack, a neighbor of my sister's.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your sister also there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my sister was there too.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in this hiding place of your mother's?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember exactly. But when it got bad, and he came, I went there to see what my mother is doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you? You were also in hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I went to the forest.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in hiding in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Wisnicz, a few kilometers-- that was approximately 10 kilometers, 10, 12 kilometers from Wisnicz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was hiding with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- that was-- that happened two days after, right away. And I went to see what my mother, and he said, you have to take your mother out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we connected. That was September. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Beginning of September. In '42?

speaker: interviewer
text: The order had been to go to Bochnia?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, the order had to be-- to everybody has to get together in Wisnicz and then come to Bochnia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they collected the Jews in Wisnicz and brought them to Bochnia.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Wisnicz and all around the vicinity, from all around, you know, people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your family tried to avoid this order.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you avoid it? What information did you have that this was not a good thing to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew. We had the feelings that it can't be good, because we heard what happened before.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you hear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they took people to Belzec, Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before '42?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anybody who was taken to Belzec or to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, nobody came back from there to tell us. But the rumors and everything, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the rumors?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were told that they're killing people, burning people. Exactly, we didn't know. Well, exactly I didn't know anything until 1943, until I actually came to Auschwitz. But then we were there, and we connected. I took my mother out. And I connect with my wife's parents. We didn't know what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother have any of her other children with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the only one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were the little children?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were in the Bochnia ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were separated from your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took them to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: They had been taken to work. Had there been any actions in Wisnicz before it was taken to ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They used to take people to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans, to work. The Bochnia ghetto--

speaker: interviewer
text: Soldiers? [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: --my older brother and my sisters. Because we knew in Bochnia they have-- they work on uniforms, German uniforms. That was-- in the beginning, that was a good job, because whoever worked there, he was safe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they get paid while they worked there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They got-- I don't remember. I think so, that I got-- I don't remember exactly, because I wasn't there. But they took my brother, and he had to have a way to make a living. So I-- I think so, that in the beginning they paid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get letters from your brother when he was in Bochnia before you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was six kilometers away. We had-- we were in contact. I'm glad you mentioned it because, as I said before, we connected with my in-laws. And we went to a hiding place. We saw a barn there far away, so we went there. And for water, we had to dig a hole until water came up. And I remember the day the water was yellow, and that's what we drank. We had one Polack with my mother, was hiding there by him. My father-in-law had money, so we gave him money. And he used to bring us bread. And we were there close to four-- I think three or four weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was there with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was my wife's parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Abraham Schon and Malka Schon. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --Tonia was her name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your wife-- your future wife. She wasn't your wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: My future wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother, and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll take--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were there, and then--

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll take a break now and pause for a tape change.
